title: Lesson 430
transcript: I want to talk in a little more detail now about input validation and a couple of different major ways that we can do input validation. Allow lists versus deny lists and whether we should do it client side versus server side. So let's talk through that because some useful stuff to know for the exam. So let's start with allow list versus deny list. This is two different methods that we can use to perform input validation, to validate user input and make sure that this is stuff that we want to pass on to our SQL database or store somewhere or something like that. So let's start with a deny list. A deny list is the idea that you explicitly deny certain things. So let's say, let's say we're worried about someone performing a cross-side scripting attack on our server. So in our deny list here, right? We could like deny, say the script tag. I'm oversimplifying here, but so you get the idea. We could basically say, we're going to deny the script tag. And so that would mean if anyone ever tried to send some input to our server that contained a script tag, the server would say, not allowed. And it would just either reject all that input or strip out the script tag. And at first blush, this seems brilliant, right? Because if no one, you know, if no one's allowed to submit the script tag, you can't get any JavaScript through or anything like that. And to be clear, denialists have their use, but they're not considered to be the most secure way of doing this for forming input validation. And here's why. Attackers are incredibly good attackers are incredibly creative people and a lot of them have all the time in the world to try every single possible combination and So it's not impossible for us as defenders to think about every single thing that we should explicitly deny Like let me give you one just stupid example. Let's say our denialist is case-sensitive All the attacker has to do to get through our deny list is they could just change the S on script to be an uppercase S and boom, there we go. They just got through our deny list by capitalizing one of the letters in the script. So that's the problem with denialists because what we're doing in a denialist is we're explicitly denying certain things. And attackers are gonna find ways around that. And we as defenders just can't think of every possible thing that we should deny. There are use cases for this, but it's not considered to be as secure. Because a denialist means you're denying certain things and you're letting everything else through. Allow lists are the opposite. In an allow list, you're explicitly allowing certain things through and everything else is denied. So for example, here's what a really secure allow list filter would look like. You might allow through just alphanumeric. So like A through Z lowercase, A through Z uppercase, and maybe 0 through 9. So you might just let through alphanumeric. Now, is anyone getting a SQL injection attack through or cross-scripting attack through this allow list filter? No, like almost no chance, right? Because the attacker cannot use a single special character here. No braces, no apostrophe, no quotes, like nothing. All they can use is uppercase A, you know, upper through lowercase, A through Z and zero through nine, just alphanumeric. That's all you're allowing through and everything else is denied. So, this would be a very restrictive list of what's allowed through. And if you're an attacker, you're not getting an attack through this. So, that's why allow lists are considered to be more secure because you're only explicitly allowing certain things through and everything else is denied. Obviously, on the exam, if you had to pick between allow list and deny list, which is more secure? Allow list. By the way, In the industry, these terms historically have often been, you'll hear the term whitelist referred to this, and this will be a blacklist. There's not great connotations attached to those words. These are much better words that we should be using, allow list and deny list. They're clear on what exactly is happening and they're just better words to be using. But the reason I point out whitelist, blacklist is you still may see that terminology on the exams. In case you come across it, yes, an allow list is the same thing as a whitelist. And Denialist is the same thing as a blacklist, but use these terms. They're better. Okay. Now, the other thing I want to talk about here is where should this input validation be done? Client side or server side? Which is more secure? And the answer is unequivocally server-side. Server-side is way more secure. Let me explain. There's nothing wrong with input validation on the client side for usability purposes. Let's say you're asking someone, like you have an online form on your website, and you're asking someone to submit their email address. You can have really simple JavaScript code that will validate, hey, is this a valid email address, right? Because every email address follows a very standard format. Some characters, an at symbol, some more characters, a period, some more characters, right? Every email address follows that very simple. format and so you can very quickly do some validation to make sure hey is this a valid email address so there's nothing wrong with client-side validation from his usability perspective but it is useless useless from a security perspective Why? Well, because it's incredibly easy to bypass. Most client-side validation will be say JavaScript in a web browser. If I'm the attacker, I can trivially easily bypass your client-side input validation by just going into settings on my browser and just disabling JavaScript. If I disable JavaScript on my browser, Boom, all the client side validation is just gone. All right, you know, and if it's even a more skilled attacker, they can just completely bypass all of your client side code and just figure out what are the get and post requests to send data directly to your server and just completely bypass anything on the client side. So just to reiterate, client-side validation from a security perspective is useless. What you always, always, always, always want to do is do input validation on the server side. It always needs to be the server that's performing the input validation. So just to sum this up, If you've got a question asking you which is the most secure way of performing input validation, you want allow list, server side.
